content.problemStatement:
Our Chef has some students in his coding class who are practicing problems. Given the difficulty of the problems that the students have solved in order, help the Chef identify if they are solving them in non-decreasing order of difficulty. Non-decreasing means that the values in an array is either increasing or remaining the same, but not decreasing. That is, the students should not solve a problem with difficulty d1d_1d1​, and then later a problem with difficulty d2d_2d2​, where d1>d2d_1 > d_2d1​>d2​.

Output “Yes” if the problems are attempted in non-decreasing order of difficulty rating and “No” if not.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases. The description of the test cases follows.
Each test case consists of 222 lines of input.

The first line contains a single integer NNN, the number of problems solved by the students
The second line contains NNN space-separate integers, the difficulty ratings of the problems attempted by the students in order.




Output Format

For each test case, output in a new line “Yes” if the problems are attempted in non-decreasing order of difficulty rating and “No” if not. The output should be printed without the quotes.

Each letter of the output may be printed in either lowercase or uppercase. For example, the strings `yes`, `YeS`, and `YES` will all be treated as equivalent.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: 1≤2≤31 \leq 2 \leq 31≤2≤3. The students have solved problems in increasing order, and so the answer is "Yes".
Test case 222: 1≤1≤21 \leq 1 \leq 21≤1≤2. The students have solved problems in non-decreasing order, and so the answer is "Yes".
Test case 333: 400>350400 \gt 350400>350, but the students have solved a 400400400-difficulty level problem before solving a 350350350-difficulty problem. The students have not solved problems in non-decreasing order, and so the answer is "No".
Test case 444: 5000>30005000 \gt 30005000>3000, but the students have solved a 500050005000-difficulty level problem before solving a 300030003000-difficulty problem. The students have not solved problems in non-decreasing order, and so the answer is "No".
